editor_note: The prose is meaning-faithful throughout: I read the entire chemistry/excited-states/max-cut tail (the most semantically dense 40%) against the English and found NO drift, inverted conditions, swapped quantities, dropped caveats, or hallucinations. The cautionary-message reasoning on the excited-state energy (orthogonality with an imperfect ground state, "no variational calculation yields energy below the true ground state", the betas-too-small explanation at line 789) is all rendered accurately and teaches as clearly as the source. What pushes this to FAIL is within-file terminology inconsistency on core domain terms, which crosses the rubric's ≥3 threshold: (1) "observable" is rendered three different ways — المؤثر (lines 26, 32, 438), المرصود (line 883, chemistry section), and مؤثِّر قابل للرصد (lines 1197, 1242, max-cut section); (2) "variational" is the file's established convention تغايري/تغايرية everywhere including headings, but the final cautionary section switches to التبايني/التبايُنية (lines 1154, 1156) for the same concept; (3) "backend" is kept as English "backend" in most places (lines 476, 513, 795) but rendered الخلفية at line 1068. Additionally "statevector overlap" is garbled at line 651 as التداخل بين حالات الانتزاعة, where الانتزاعة is not a quantum term and obscures that statevector is meant. None of these inverts meaning, but a learner meeting المرصود and التبايني could reasonably wonder whether a new concept is being introduced rather than the already-named المؤثر and الخوارزمية التغايرية.

examples.why: "observable" appears under three different Arabic terms (المؤثر, المرصود, مؤثِّر قابل للرصد) within one file; a learner cannot tell these denote the same core object.

editor_note: This is meaning-faithful, well-edited Arabic technical prose that teaches as clearly as the English. Core domain terms are rendered with correct, consistent conventions: كيوبت (qubit), بوابة هادامارد (Hadamard gate), تراكب (superposition), تداخل (interference), متوازنة/ثابتة (balanced/constant), نموذج الاستعلام و الصندوق الأسود (query model / black box). I checked every drift-prone passage in the last 40% — the BV dot-product-modulo-2 setup (line 566), the speedup claims (أسي exponential vs متعدد الحدود polynomial vs فائق التعدد الحدودي super-polynomial at lines 561/713/715), Deutsch's interference condition (0 if both same → constant, 1 if different → balanced, line 254), and the histogram/collapse explanation (lines 210-212) — and all conditions, quantities, and caveats match the source with no inversion or hallucination. The intended informal register holds (تفضّل، ممكن تشوف، ستشوف؛ no formal antum). The only blemishes are cosmetic and explicitly out of scope here: "phase kickback" is rendered three ways (الارتداد الطوري line 315, the literal English phase-kickback line 390, الرفس الطوري lines 584/602/723/740) and "Jozsa" is transliterated two ways (جوزسا and يوزسا) — neither misleads a learner, so the file is a clean semantic PASS.

The translation is fluent, faithful technical Arabic with correct and fully consistent domain terminology: "مولّد المثبّت" (stabilizer generator), "كيوبت" (qubit), "تكافؤ" (parity), "متلازمة" (syndrome), "خطأ منطقي" (logical error), "غير تافه" (nontrivial) and "طارة/طارية" (torus/toric) are all used uniformly throughout. The critical quantitative and parity-based claims that a learner could be misled by are all preserved correctly: even-vs-odd parity outcomes (lines 113, 121-122), the encode-count $2L^2-2(L^2-1)=2$ (87), the two loop categories (even on every line → in stabilizer; odd on at least one line → undetected, 147-149), distance $L$ and the 10% noise-rate caveat (161, 202-203). No inversions, swapped quantities, or hallucinations. The only defects are two DROPPED pedagogical clarifications, both in the loop-classification paragraph: (1) the parenthetical defining what a "horizontal/vertical lattice line" means (a full row/column, NOT the loop segments) from EN line 145 is omitted entirely, and (2) the explanatory clause in category 1 — "since each lattice line crosses the closed loop of errors once... and once crossing back out" (EN 147) — is collapsed to just "(المثال الموضح أعلاه يقع في هذه الفئة.)", removing the reasoning that explains the even parity. These flatten teaching nuance but do not invert or falsify any claim, so this is MINOR, not FAIL. Informal register is consistent; minor grammatical stiffness ("لأن حدث" for "because... occurred" in 121-122, "تبيان مختلف" in 186) is cosmetic.

editor_note: This is native, well-edited Czech technical prose that teaches as clearly as the English source. Domain terminology is correct and consistent throughout: "dotazovací hradlo" for query gate, "fázové dotazovací hradlo" for phase query gate, "kvantový obvod" for quantum circuit, "registr"/"qubit"/"standardní báze", "Booleovský obvod" for Boolean circuit, and "jev fázového zpětného rázu (phase kickback)" sensibly keeps the English term in parentheses as a gloss. Meaning is faithful paragraph-by-paragraph: the phase-kickback workspace-qubit caveat (reused/"znovu použit" vs discarded/"zahozen"), the cost comparison (Z_f one query / Z_OR no queries), and the last-section if/else search logic (f(x)=1 → stop and output x; f(x)=0 → rerun or give up) are all preserved with no inversion, swapped quantity, or dropped caveat. The closing claim "t = O(√N) ... (pokud existuje) s vysokou pravděpodobností" keeps both the "if one exists" hedge and "with high probability." Informal imperative register ("Všimni si", "Zvol", "Spusť", "Dotázej se", "abys zjistil") holds consistently with no formal Vy slips.

This reads like genuine Czech technical prose written by someone who understands the physics, not raw MT: the coin/superposition analogy, the phase/interference explanation, and the Bloch-vector conclusion all teach with the same intuition and emphasis as the English, and the informal "ty" register (Nauč se, Všimni si, Chceš-li, zkus) is held consistently. Core terminology is correct and conventional — qubit, superpozice, Blochův vektor, fáze, střední hodnota (glossed once as "expectation value"), pozorovatelná veličina, provázanost (entanglement), and Sampler/Estimator/backend kept in English. Crucially for meaning: every negation, quantity and caveat in the back half survives intact — "**není** tu 50% pravděpodobnost" (line 333), "vždy jako $|+\rangle$ a nikdy jako $|-\rangle$" (553), the suggestive bold claim, and the closing "ověření toho vyžaduje měření!" (696) are all faithful, so a learner is not misled about any physics. The three minor flaws are localization/consistency slips, not semantic drift: (1) the gambling example localizes "dollar" to "euro" in the prose (line 195: "vyhraješ jedno euro") while the math block and line 201 still say "1 dollar"/"$0" — a prose-vs-formula currency mismatch but the expectation value is still 0, so no conceptual error; (2) "gate" is rendered as "hradlo" in most of the file but as "brána" throughout the second-Hadamard section (lines 377–404, 454–456) — both are valid Czech, but the within-file flip is noticeable; (3) line 61 switches the coin idiom from the líc/rub used elsewhere to "počet orlů a pán" (with "pán" a grammatical slip for "panen"). None of these mislead; they keep this just short of a clean PASS.

editor_note: This is overall faithful, fluent, pedagogically-sound technical French that reads like a quantum physicist wrote it. Core terminology is correct and consistent: "valeur d'espérance" (expectation value), "état propre"/"états propres" (eigenstate), "matrices de Pauli", "relation d'incertitude", "écart-type" (standard deviation), "quantité de mouvement" (momentum), "porte Hadamard", "commutateur"/"ne commutent pas", "post-traitement", "vecteur d'état"; English domain terms are appropriately kept (qubit, backend, Sampler, Estimator, job, package, little-endian, Qiskit patterns, pubs). Informal "tu" register holds cleanly throughout (consulte, assure-toi, choisis, t'attendrais-tu). The one genuine meaning concern is an inverted swap instruction in the last accordion answer (~FR L899-905): the English says to swap "qc.rz(pi/2,0)" IN to replace "qc.rz(pi/4,0)" (so the new code uses pi/2), but the French "qc.rz(pi/2,0) remplacé par qc.rz(pi/4,0)" literally reads as pi/2 being replaced by pi/4 — the opposite direction, which contradicts the preceding answer that correctly changes pi/4→pi/2 to maximize ⟨Y⟩. The source phrasing ("swapped in to replace") is itself loose, and this is an isolated slip, so it lands as MINOR rather than FAIL. A secondary, non-misleading note: "layout of the quantum computer" became "topologie" (~L159), a slight shift from layout to topology that is defensible in a transpilation context.

examples.source: We would use exactly the code from the example above, with `qc.rz(pi/2,0)` swapped in to replace `qc.rz(pi/4,0)`.
examples.translation: Nous utiliserions exactement le code de l'exemple ci-dessus, avec `qc.rz(pi/2,0)` remplacé par `qc.rz(pi/4,0).`
examples.suggested: Nous utiliserions exactement le code de l'exemple ci-dessus, en remplaçant `qc.rz(pi/4,0)` par `qc.rz(pi/2,0)`.
examples.why: The French inverts the replacement direction: 'A remplacé par B' means A is replaced by B (result = pi/4), but the English intent — confirmed by the preceding accordion answer — is to put pi/2 in place of pi/4 to maximize ⟨Y⟩. A learner following the French would revert to pi/4 and defeat the exercise.

examples.source: It also maps our problem onto the layout of the quantum computer.
examples.translation: Elle mappe également notre problème sur la topologie de l'ordinateur quantique.
examples.suggested: Elle mappe également notre problème sur l'agencement (layout) de l'ordinateur quantique.
examples.why: 'layout' (qubit-to-physical assignment chosen by the transpiler) was rendered as 'topologie', which technically refers to the fixed coupling map. Not misleading enough to fail, but a mild conceptual shift in teaching material.

editor_note: This reads as well-edited native French technical prose, not machine translation — choices like "un outil précieux en cryptographie", "à ce titre", "brique fondamentale" (for "fundamental building block"), and "remplacement direct" (drop-in replacement) are idiomatic decisions a French crypto writer would make. Core domain terminology is correct and used consistently throughout: "condensat (ou digest)" for digest, "fonction à sens unique" (one-way function), "force brute" (brute force), "somme de contrôle" (checksum), "attaque des anniversaires" (birthday attack), "pré-image", "effet avalanche", and "plusieurs-à-un"/"un-à-un" for many-to-one/one-to-one. Meaning is fully faithful in every quantitative and conditional claim I checked: the $2^n$ / $2^{n/2}$ / $2^{n-1}$ complexities, the "better than 50% chance" ("plus de 50 % de chances"), the 256-bit-equals-128-bit Grover statement, and — most importantly for a teaching file — the subtle caveats survive intact (Grover gives no extra speedup on collisions beyond the birthday bound and "ne constitue pas une menace pratique"; BHT's $O(2^{n/3})$ is conditioned on QRAM "qui n'existe pas encore", with the without-QRAM $\tilde{O}(2^{2n/5})$ being only marginal; SHA-3 "resteront probablement sécurisés, sauf découverte d'attaques cryptanalytiques encore inconnues" keeps the hedge). No inverted negation, no swapped quantity, no softened/strengthened claim, no hallucinated sentence. The register question (tu vs vous) does not arise — the text uses impersonal "on" and infinitives throughout, matching the English's neutral expository voice.

This reads as genuine Hebrew technical prose written by someone fluent in the domain, not machine output. Core terminology is correct and fully consistent: "אנזאץ'" (ansatz), "פונקציית עלות" (cost function), "מייעל" (optimizer), "מצב ייחוס"/"מצב המטרה"/"מצב ברירת המחדל" (reference/target/default state), "המילטוניאן" (Hamiltonian), "ערך עצמי"/"מצב עצמי" (eigenvalue/eigenstate), "פירוק ספקטרלי" (spectral decomposition), "אופרטורי Pauli", and "מתכנס לפתרון אופטימלי" (converges on an optimal solution). I read the high-risk last 40% — the variational-theorem statement and the mathematical-assumptions block — paragraph against paragraph: the finite-lower-bound caveat ("חסם תחתון סופי לאנרגיה ... אפילו עבור N→∞"), the "upper bounds do not generally exist" note, and the generalization-by-swapping-bounds argument (minimize↔maximize, lower↔upper) are all preserved with the correct direction and no dropped conditions, no inverted negations, and no hallucinated claims. Two purely cosmetic choices worth noting but NOT defects: "observable" is transliterated as "אובייקטיבל" rather than "גודל נצפה", and "expectation value" rendered as "ערך הציפייה" rather than "ערך התוחלת" — both are defensible, internally consistent engineering-register conventions. The instructional register stays appropriately informal ("נחקור", "עלינו להגדיר", "שימו לב") and teaches the energy/ground-state intuition as clearly as the English.

This reads as natural, fluent Hebrew technical prose with the correct informal register (plural "תוכלו", "שלכם", "מלאו") and well-chosen domain terminology that is used consistently: "אלכסון מטריצות" for matrix diagonalization, "ערכי ציפייה" for expectation values, "תת-מרחבים" for subspaces, "להטיל"/"המטריצה המוטלת" for project/projected matrix, and "סבילות לתקלות בקנה מידה גדול" for large-scale fault tolerance — all conventional and accurate. Core English-kept terms (VQE, SQD, Qiskit Runtime primitives) are sensibly left untranslated, matching field convention. Meaning is faithful paragraph by paragraph, including all quantities (20-item quiz, 70% threshold, "at least") and the eight learning goals. The only minor semantic slip is in paragraph 3, where "what factors determine the scaling of approaches" is rendered as "הגורמים הקובעים את יעילות הגישות" ("efficiency" rather than "scaling"); this narrows the concept slightly but does not mislead the learner, since the surrounding sentence about whether a problem benefits from a quantum algorithm is fully preserved. No hallucinations, no inverted conditions, no dropped caveats — this teaches as clearly as the English.

examples.source: There is substantial discussion of what factors determine the scaling of approaches using classical and quantum algorithms.
examples.translation: ישנו דיון מעמיק בגורמים הקובעים את יעילות הגישות תוך שימוש באלגוריתמים קלאסיים וקוונטיים.
examples.suggested: ...בגורמים הקובעים את התנהגות הסקיילינג (ההתרחבות) של הגישות...
examples.why: "scaling" was rendered as "יעילות" (efficiency); scaling and efficiency are related but distinct concepts. Minor conceptual narrowing, not misleading in context — kept as the single noted issue, verdict remains PASS.

editor_note: Meaning is faithful end-to-end. This is a management/strategy lesson (vision, mission, goals, focus areas) with little hard quantum terminology, and every load-bearing claim and quantity survives translation: the 4-step early-stage sequence, "at most two dimensions" (לכל היותר), "non-zero value to all four dimensions" (ערך מסוים שאינו אפס), the "breadth can hinder success" caveat, and the two example value-maps (priorities1 business+scientific, priorities2 business+eminence) all match their setups. No inverted negation, swapped quantity, softened claim, or hallucination — including in the last 40% (focus-area and client-engagement sections). The flags are naturalness/register, not meaning: two raw transliterations a native technical writer would never use — "פיסקל" for "fiscal" (should be תקציביים/כספיים, line 12) and "פרסקריפטיבית" for "prescriptive" (line 14) — plus an inconsistent and nonstandard plural for "dimensions" (מימדות vs the correct מימדים, both within line 35), and a calqued/ungrammatical construction "לא יכול להיות להשפעה עסקית גדולה" (line 50, should be לא יכולה להיות השפעה). Register is appropriately informal (imperatives הגדר/זהה/בנה). None of these mislead a learner, so MINOR not FAIL.

examples.source: including research goals, and human and fiscal resources
examples.translation: כולל יעדי מחקר, ומשאבי אנוש ופיסקל
examples.suggested: כולל יעדי מחקר, ומשאבי אנוש ומשאבים תקציביים
examples.why: "fiscal" is left as the bare transliteration "פיסקל", not a Hebrew word a native technical writer would use; natural term is תקציביים/כספיים. Meaning recoverable, so naturalness slip only.

examples.source: through a clear and prescriptive mission and objectives
examples.translation: דרך משימה ברורה ופרסקריפטיבית ויעדים
examples.suggested: דרך משימה ברורה ומנחה (מכוונת) ויעדים
examples.why: "prescriptive" rendered as transliteration "פרסקריפטיבית" rather than a real Hebrew rendering (מנחה/מכוונת/מורה). Reads as untranslated MT but does not change meaning.

examples.source: Four dimensions in particular have proved useful... We recommend prioritizing at most two dimensions
examples.translation: ארבעה מימדות בפרט הוכיחו... לתת עדיפות לשני מימדות לכל היותר
examples.suggested: ארבעה מימדים בפרט הוכיחו... לשני מימדים לכל היותר
examples.why: "dimensions" pluralized as nonstandard "מימדות" (correct: מימדים), inconsistently within the file. Quantity ("at most two" = לכל היותר) is correct, so consistency/naturalness, not drift.

editor_note: This is a faithful, naturally-written Indonesian rendering of a short Qiskit code-walkthrough lesson. Terminology is correct and internally consistent: "perkalian tensor" for tensor product, "vektor keadaan"/`Statevector` for state vector, "keadaan gabungan" for compound states, "qubit" left untranslated, and conventional API/physics terms (control, target, keadaan Bell) handled appropriately. The semantically load-bearing directional details all survive: the tensor argument is "faktor tensor di sebelah kanan" (right factor), the spin caveat correctly keeps the negation ("ini tidak merujuk pada qubit paling kanan atau paling kiri"), and the qubit-numbering note preserves "mulai dari 0, dari kanan ke kiri" (from 0, right to left) plus "qubit paling kanan" for qubit 0. The CX description correctly assigns left=control, right=target and names the result as the Bell state. Register is informal ("kita", "Anda"-style, no deferential bapak/ibu), the prose reads like a native technical writer, and no claims were softened, strengthened, inverted, or hallucinated.

This is a strong, meaning-faithful translation that reads like a native Indonesian quantum-information writer wrote it; I found no semantic drift or hallucination across either half, including the dense last 40% (unitary equivalence, superdense coding, bit commitment, and the Hughston-Jozsa-Wootters proof). Core terminology is consistently correct and conventional: "matriks densitas", "vektor keadaan kuantum", "nilai eigen / vektor eigen", "keadaan tereduksi", "dekomposisi spektral", "bebas linear", "operasi unitari / kesetaraan unitari", with field terms appropriately kept in English ("qubit", "partial trace", "trace out", "bit commitment", "binding/concealing/commit/reveal", "e-bit", "Bell state"). Conditions, negations, quantifiers and worked-example details (e.g. "necessary and sufficient" → "diperlukan dan cukup", the N=1.000.000 example, the U/U† commitment-flip directions) are all preserved accurately. The single defect is a within-file terminology inconsistency: "convex combination" is rendered "kombinasi konveks" three times (lines ~46, ~59, ~130) but "kombinasi cembung" once (line ~472) — both are valid Indonesian and do not mislead the learner, so this is a MINOR consistency slip, not a meaning problem.

examples.source: ...as a convex combination of pure states... (recurring)
examples.translation: line ~472: "...sebagai kombinasi cembung dari keadaan murni..." vs line ~46/~59/~130: "...kombinasi konveks..."
examples.suggested: Use "kombinasi konveks" consistently (matching the majority usage) in place of "kombinasi cembung" at line ~472
examples.why: "convex combination" is the same domain term throughout; rendering it as both "kombinasi konveks" and "kombinasi cembung" is an internal inconsistency. It does not mislead (both are correct Indonesian), so it stays at MINOR level.

editor_note: This is native, well-edited Indonesian business-technical prose that reads as written, not machine-translated; sentence rhythm and connectives ("seiring kemajuan teknologi", "dalam antisipasi", "berusaha menyelesaikan") are idiomatic and the informal register holds consistently with "kamu"/"-mu" (lines 13, 32, 57, 134, 136) and no deferential bapak/ibu. Terminology is correct and consistent: "komputasi kuantum", "komputer kuantum", "kernel kuantum", "observabel termodinamika" for thermodynamic observables, "ilmu hayat" for life sciences, "simulasi Monte Carlo", and field-standard English kept untranslated where appropriate ("machine learning", "last mile", "quantum ready", "net zero"). Critically, the load-bearing quantitative claim in Financial Services is faithful: "Mengurangi kesalahan ... sebesar faktor 1/X memerlukan X^2 lebih banyak sampel tradisional, tetapi hanya X lebih banyak sampel kuantum" preserves both exponents and the two-way interpretation (fixed confidence faster vs. more confidence for fixed time). No inverted negations, swapped quantities, dropped caveats, or hallucinated assertions were found in any section, including the dense last 40% (manufacturing, ExxonMobil/Mitsubishi, utilities, key takeaways). The only nits are isolated flattenings that do not mislead: "melampaui daya komputasi superkomputer terbesar saat ini" softens English "beyond the current computing power of even today's supercomputers" (uses "terbesar"/largest instead of conveying "even"), and "juara komputasi kuantum" is a literal calque of "quantum computing champion" where a writer might prefer "penggerak" — both remain understandable.

examples.source: Simulating all the various molecular properties and behaviors is beyond the current computing power of even today's supercomputers.
examples.translation: Mensimulasikan semua berbagai sifat dan perilaku molekuler melampaui daya komputasi superkomputer terbesar saat ini.
examples.suggested: ... melampaui daya komputasi bahkan superkomputer tercanggih saat ini.
examples.why: English 'even today's supercomputers' emphasizes that the most capable current machines still cannot do this; ID renders it as 'largest' (terbesar), slightly flattening the 'even the best can't' emphasis. Meaning is preserved, not misleading.

examples.source: identifying a quantum computing champion
examples.translation: mengidentifikasi juara komputasi kuantum
examples.suggested: menunjuk penggerak/penanggung jawab komputasi kuantum
examples.why: 'juara' is a literal calque of 'champion'; the business sense (an internal advocate/sponsor) is recoverable from context but a native technical writer would likely use 'penggerak' or keep 'champion'. Minor, not misleading.

editor_note: This is a meaning-faithful, well-edited translation that teaches as clearly as the English; I found no semantic drift, inverted negation, swapped quantity, dropped caveat, or hallucination across the whole file, including the dense reasoning passages in the last 40%. The critical binary-logic paragraph (L641: "se una tale linea orizzontale *non* viene rilevata, allora si tratta di una linea verticale") and the CNOT qubit indices (0-1,1-2,2-3 / 4-5,5-6,6-7, and the q35/q34/q30/q31 reorder discussion at L1029) are all preserved exactly, as are the quantities ("già circa il 60% più bassa", "72 parametri liberi", "fattore 2-3"). Core terminology is correct and conventional: "osservabile", "valore di aspettazione", and the kept-in-English "ansatz", "feature map", "backend", "barren plateau"; informal "tu" register holds throughout with no Lei slips. The only blemishes are two minor naturalness/consistency slips that do not mislead: at L609 "sembra abbastanza converso" uses an awkward past participle a native technical writer would more likely render as "sembra abbastanza convergente"/"ha raggiunto la convergenza", and "transpile" is rendered inconsistently as "transpilare/transpilazione" (L693, L1090, L1120) but "trasposizione" once at L1029 (alongside a parallel "layer"/"livello" mix). These are cosmetic, so the verdict is MINOR_ISSUES rather than PASS, and nowhere near FAIL.

examples.source: But it looks fairly converged.
examples.translation: Ma sembra abbastanza converso.
examples.suggested: Ma sembra abbastanza convergente.
examples.why: "converso" is an unusual/awkward past participle of "convergere" in this context; a native quantum/ML writer would say "convergente" or "ha raggiunto una buona convergenza". Meaning is intact, so this is a cosmetic slip only.

examples.source: it isn't obvious that this will make a difference after transpilation
examples.translation: non è ovvio che questo faccia differenza dopo la trasposizione
examples.suggested: ...dopo la transpilazione
examples.why: "transpile" is otherwise consistently rendered "transpilare/transpilato/transpilazione" (L693, L1090, L1120); the lone "trasposizione" here is an inconsistency. Not misleading (clear from context) but a within-file terminology slip.

editor_note: This is faithful, fluent technical Italian that teaches as clearly as the English. Core domain terminology is correct and internally consistent: "hamiltoniano/Hamiltoniano", "stato fondamentale" (ground state), "valore di aspettazione" (expectation value), "funzione d'onda di prova" (trial wave function), "set di basi"/"base" (basis set), "ottimizzatore classico", "energia di reazione"; field-conventional English is rightly kept untranslated ("ansatz/ansätze", "qubit", "backend", "observable", "Estimator", "transpilazione", "QPU", "shots", "Qiskit patterns", "barren plateaus" rendered as "altopiani sterili"). I checked every high-risk meaning point in the last 40% — the variational inequality ("sempre uguale o superiore"), the "energia misurata leggermente inferiore al valore teorico esatto" caveat, the negative reaction energy meaning "l'energia viene rilasciata", the true/false negation #5 ("non influisce"), MCQ-6A "altrimenti fallirà", and the Coulomb-law unit conversion (0.735·1.88973=1.38895) — all preserve direction, quantity, and caveats with no drift or hallucination. The informal "tu" convention holds throughout ("Considera", "Immagina", "sentiti libero", "Puoi vedere") with no Lei slips. Only cosmetic stiffness exists (e.g. "Questi sono attesi per migliorare" calquing "These are expected to enhance"), which does not mislead, so this is a clean PASS.

examples.source: These are expected to enhance materials discovery, drug design, and reaction predictions
examples.translation: Questi sono attesi per migliorare la scoperta di materiali, la progettazione di farmaci e le previsioni di reazione
examples.suggested: Si prevede che questi migliorino la scoperta di materiali, la progettazione di farmaci e le previsioni delle reazioni
examples.why: Calque of the English passive 'are expected to'; a native writer would use 'Si prevede che...'. Meaning is fully intact, so this is cosmetic only and not a defect that changes sense.

editor_note: This reads like native Italian technical mathematics, not machine translation: core domain terminology is correct and perfectly consistent throughout — "prodotto interno" (inner product), "trasposto coniugato" (conjugate transpose), "norma euclidea", "base/insieme ortonormale", "matrice unitaria", "matrici Hermitiane / idempotenti", "misurazione proiettiva" vs "misurazione nella base standard", with "qubit" and "vettore unitario" rendered conventionally. Idiomatic choices like "vettori che vivono in uno spazio n-dimensionale", "definitezza positiva", "matrice a blocchi" and "se e solo se" are exactly what an Italian quantum/math writer would use. Semantics are faithful paragraph by paragraph, including the dense final 40% (the M block-matrix construction, the orthonormality-of-the-first-n-columns argument, and the closing statement that "Y memorizza una copia del risultato … X cambia esattamente come avrebbe fatto se … fosse stata eseguita direttamente su X"); all quantities, conditions, negations and caveats (zero-matrix empty-sum special case, m≤n bound, "a meno che m=1") are preserved. Register holds informal/first-person-plural ("introduciamo", "supponiamo", imperative "Nota che…"), no Lei slips. The only fidelity nuance: at the step "When we perform a projective measurement on Y" the Italian says "misurazione nella base standard su Y" — but since the implemented operation is precisely a standard-basis measurement on Y (as stated two lines earlier), this is technically equivalent and not misleading, so it does not lower the verdict.

This reads like native Japanese technical prose written by someone fluent in quantum computing, not machine translation. Core terminology is conventional and internally consistent: 量子ビット (qubit), 重ね合わせ (superposition), 干渉パターン (interference patterns), クエリゲート/クエリモデル (query gate/model), 標準基底状態 (standard basis states), ブール回路 (Boolean circuit), and 約束問題 (promise problem) are all the correct domain renderings and used uniformly throughout. The "needle in a haystack" idiom is naturally localized as 干し草の山の中から針を探す. Crucially for meaning, every quantitative and conditional claim is faithful: the N vs O(N) vs O(√N) distinctions, the deterministic-best-case vs probabilistic claims, the "evaluations must happen in superposition" emphasis, the "(if one exists)" caveat (解が存在する場合は), the "not a promise problem" negation (約束問題ではない), and the unique-search promise (ちょうど一つ存在し、x≠z のすべてに対して f(x)=0) all match the source exactly with no drift, no inversion, and nothing hallucinated. Register is consistent desu/masu with no keigo. This teaches the unstructured-search setup as clearly as the English.

This reads as native, well-edited Japanese technical prose written by someone fluent in quantum error correction, not machine translation. Core terminology is correct and used consistently throughout: 安定化演算子生成元 (stabilizer generator), 量子誤り訂正符号, 表面符号/回転表面符号 (surface/rotated surface code), カラー符号, トーリック符号, スティーン符号 (Steane code), 三彩色 (three-colorable), and 耐故障量子計算 (fault-tolerant quantum computation) — all defensible conventional renderings. I verified the meaning-critical claims paragraph against paragraph, with special attention to the last 40%: the X/Z error-chain directions (left-right vs top-bottom) are mapped correctly (L26), the surface code encoding "a single qubit, rather than two" is preserved (L23), the black=X / white=Z tile mapping and "two or four vertices" is intact (L33), the colors-don't-matter-for-definition-but-matter-for-analysis caveat is fully preserved (L73), and every Gross-code quantity ($[[144,12,12]]$, weight 6, two additional qubits, 12 vs 2 encoded) is faithful (L82). No semantic drift, inverted conditions, dropped caveats, or hallucinations found. Register is consistent informal desu/masu with no keigo slips. The only trivial polish nit (not misleading and outside this run's scope) is mixing katakana エラー with the otherwise-preferred 誤り in "エラーの連鎖"; this does not affect meaning.

This reads like native Japanese technical prose written by someone fluent in quantum-algorithm vocabulary, not machine translation. Core domain terms are correct and consistent throughout: 量子クエリアルゴリズム, アダマールゲート, クエリゲート, 位相キックバック (phase kickback), 零空間 (null space), ガウス消去法 (Gaussian elimination), 値域 (range), 逆像 (preimage), 約束問題 (promise problem), and the if-and-only-if 約束 condition rendered as 必要十分条件. The meaning-critical passages in the last 40% are all faithful: the negation in "s is *not* the exclusive-OR of any two queried strings" is correctly carried by ではない (line 297); the boundary claims "fewer than 2^{n/2-1}-1 queries" (回未満), "at least 1/2 probability" (少なくとも 1/2), and "at least 1-2^{-r}" (少なくとも) all preserve direction and quantity; and the closing linear-vs-exponential contrast keeps its *線形*/*指数的* emphasis. One defensible terminology note, not flagged as a defect: "one-to-one function" is rendered 全単射 (bijective) rather than strictly 単射 (injective), applied consistently (lines 43/161/185); given the author's framing of f as a bijection onto its 2^n-element range, this does not mislead the learner about the operative fact. Register holds (です/ます, plain でください imperatives, no keigo).

This is a short course-overview page (marketing/legal prose, no math or deep technical content), and the Korean reads as natural, well-edited technical Korean rather than raw MT. Core terminology is correct and consistent throughout: "quantum-safe cryptography" → 양자 안전 암호, and the four cryptography areas render to the conventional Korean terms (암호 해시 함수, 대칭 키 암호, 비대칭 키 암호, 양자 안전 암호); "primer" → 입문서 is the right register. Meaning is fully faithful paragraph-by-paragraph: the exam caveat ("intended to be taken after reading the lessons" → 수업을 읽은 후에 치르도록 설계되었습니다) keeps its condition, Credly is correctly named as the notifying agent, and the dense Credly legal paragraph preserves every claim (third-party processor authorized by IBM and located in the US, the shared fields name/email/badge, and "may share globally with IBM subsidiaries and third parties"). The only trivial flattening is "poised to shape the quantum era" → 양자 시대를 형성할 (the "poised to" nuance is mild), which does not mislead. Register is consistent 합니다체/해요체 with no over-honorific 하십시오체 or humble 드리 slips. No semantic drift, no inverted negation, no dropped caveat, no hallucinated content.

This reads like native Korean technical writing, not machine translation: sentences are restructured idiomatically (e.g. the password caveat is split into a clean two-clause "...메커니즘이 바람직합니다. 비밀번호를 직접 다루면 기밀성이 침해될 수 있기 때문입니다." rather than a calqued relative clause), and the 해요체 register holds consistently with no 하십시오체/드리 slips. Core cryptographic terminology is correct and used consistently throughout: 다이제스트, 무차별 대입(brute force), 눈사태 효과(avalanche effect), 근사 단사성(approximate injectivity), 사전 이미지 저항성/충돌 저항성, 생일 공격, 양자 진폭 증폭. I read the delicate last 40% paragraph-against-paragraph — the quantum-threat reasoning is fully faithful: Grover's quadratic speedup → 2^{n/2} (256-bit ≡ 128-bit), the nuanced claim that Grover gives no collision advantage beyond the birthday-attack limit and so halving "does not pose a practical threat," the BHT O(2^{n/3}) scaling being predicated on not-yet-existent QRAM, and the without-QRAM Õ(2^{2n/5}) "marginal/not critical" conclusion are all preserved with correct directionality and no dropped caveats. The only nit is "후계자" for "successor to SHA-2" (slightly personifying vs. the more idiomatic 후속), but that is pure polish, not meaning drift. Faithful and pedagogically equivalent to the English.

examples.source: approximate injectivity ... the avalanche effect whereby tiny changes in the input lead to wildly divergent digests

examples.why: Correct, conventional Korean math/crypto terms (단사성 = injectivity, 눈사태 효과 = avalanche effect) used accurately and consistently; meaning fully preserved.

examples.source: the fact that Grover search effectively halves the hash length with respect to pre-image attacks does not pose a practical threat

A learner is told electrons are "insoluble" fermions, which misstates the indistinguishability that the whole fermion-to-qubit mapping motivates. Separately, the final source sentence ("You should now have an arsenal of tools…") is dropped entirely; the translation ends at the conversion paragraph (line 550).

Core domain terms (Hamiltonian, Ansatz, Classical optimizer, Estimator, primitives, Qiskit patterns) are kept in English consistently per Thai field convention, and the Qiskit-patterns bullet list (Map/Optimize/Execute/Post-process) maps one-to-one. Register stays casual (คุณ/มัน, no ครับ/ค่ะ); the injected IBM survey/Note block is expected and not a defect. The only nits are cosmetic, not semantic — "ผู้ที่ปฏิบัติงานด้านเทคนิคของการคำนวณควอนตัม" is a slightly literal calque of "technical practitioners," and "biophysical problems" is left as the bare noun "biophysics" next to the translated "ชีวเคมี" — neither misleads a learner.

This is faithful, natural Tagalog technical prose that teaches as clearly as the English. The translator correctly keeps established domain terms in English (qubit, tensor product, state vector, Bell state, control/target, compound state) while using natural Tagalog connective prose ("nagbabalik ng tensor product", "i-evolve gamit ang mga compound na operasyon"), and renders "measurement" consistently as "sukat"/"sukatin"/"masuri" and "state" as "estado" without drift. All load-bearing details survive intact: the tensor factor is "sa kanan" (on the right), the CX control/target mapping ("qubit sa kaliwa ang kontrol at ang qubit sa kanan ang target") is preserved exactly, the spin left/right caveat keeps its negation ("hindi ito tumutukoy sa pinaka-kanan o pinaka-kaliwang qubit"), and the closing measurement section correctly states qubit 0 is "pinakakanang qubit" with Qiskit numbering "mula kanan hanggang kaliwa". The restrictive "only those qubit indices" is faithfully rendered with "lamang". Register stays casual throughout (tayo/natin, no po/opo). No semantic drift, hallucination, or terminology inconsistency found.

Most of this file is faithful, fluent technical Tagalog — the entanglement narrative, the theory derivation, the Alice/Bob outcome table, and the Critical Concepts list all teach as clearly as the English, with consistent informal register and English domain terms (qubit, gate, entanglement, superposisyon, secret state, transpile, Sampler) used sensibly. But there are genuine meaning failures, not just stiffness. The worst is line 444: "simulated" is rendered as "maisimula" (root *simula* = to start/begin), TWICE, so the paragraph's whole point — that classical computers can SIMULATE this simple teleportation — collapses into a meaningless claim that computations can be "started" on classical computers; this also clashes with "simulator" correctly kept in English in the same paragraph. In the same sentence "it isn't at all surprising" became "hindi ito kabulaanan" ("this is not a falsehood/lie"), changing the claim. Separately, MC Question 1 (line 652) translates "separated by a great distance" as "pinagsamantala ng isang malaking distansya" ("exploited by a great distance") — a mistranslation in a quiz premise, and inconsistent with the correct "pinaghiwalay/ihihiwalay" used for "separate" everywhere else in the file (e.g. line 302). These mislead a learner, so FAIL despite the otherwise solid prose. Minor extra blemishes ("segretong" for secrecy at line 254, non-standard "magtulot" at line 255) are noise on top.

examples.source: Not all computations on a quantum computer can be reasonably simulated on classical computers. This simple teleportation definitely can be...
examples.translation: Hindi lahat ng computations sa isang quantum computer ay makatuwirang maisimula sa mga classical computer. Ang simpleng teleportasyong ito ay tiyak na maisimula...
examples.suggested: Hindi lahat ng computations sa isang quantum computer ay makatuwirang ma-simulate sa mga classical computer. Ang simpleng teleportasyong ito ay tiyak na kayang ma-simulate...
examples.why: 'simulated' rendered as 'maisimula' (= started/initiated, root simula), twice. The paragraph's thesis (classical computers can SIMULATE this) is lost, and it contradicts 'simulator' kept correctly in English in the same paragraph.

examples.source: but it isn't at all surprising that we can classically save information in one place or another.
examples.translation: ngunit hindi ito kabulaanan na maaari nating classical na i-save ang impormasyon sa isang lugar o iba pa.
examples.suggested: ngunit hindi naman nakakagulat na maaari nating classical na i-save ang impormasyon sa isang lugar o iba pa.
examples.why: 'isn't at all surprising' became 'hindi ito kabulaanan' (= this is not a falsehood/lie). The claim is changed, not just reworded.

examples.source: Qubits A and B are entangled, then separated by a great distance d.
examples.translation: Ang mga qubit A at B ay na-entangle, pagkatapos ay pinagsamantala ng isang malaking distansya d.
examples.suggested: Ang mga qubit A at B ay na-entangle, pagkatapos ay pinaghiwalay ng isang malaking distansya d.
examples.why: 'separated' mistranslated as 'pinagsamantala' (= exploited/taken advantage of) in a quiz premise; inconsistent with 'pinaghiwalay/ihihiwalay' used correctly for 'separate' elsewhere (line 302).

editor_note: The translation is overwhelmingly meaning-faithful, fluent technical Tagalog with consistent, correct domain conventions: it keeps English field terms (stabilizer generator, parity check, codeword, code space, dual code, qubit, bit-flip/phase-flip, Hamming code, Steane code, eigenvector) and translates only the surrounding scaffolding (klasikal na linear na code, kahulugan, pagtuklas at pagwawasto ng error), and the informal register holds throughout with no po/opo. One genuine semantic slip mars it: in the sentence explaining why Z stabilizers are insensitive to Z errors and corrections, "completely oblivious to both" is rendered "ganap silang bigo sa pareho" (line 279) — "bigo" means "failed," which inverts the teaching point (the generators do NOT fail; they are blind/indifferent to those operations). Secondary softening: the claim that each parity-check/stabilizer generator "halves / cuts in half" the code space is rendered "naghahatid ng kalahati" ("delivers half") at lines 103 and 306, which obscures the "cuts in half" action though the gist is recoverable. There is also a malformed coined verb "nispanspan" (attempted conjugation of "span," lines 369/371/392) — a naturalness defect, not a meaning error, since "span the code space" is still clear. None of these is pervasive; the bulk of the prose teaches as clearly as the English.

examples.source: so they're completely oblivious to both, and likewise for X stabilizer generators, errors, and corrections.
examples.translation: kaya ganap silang bigo sa pareho, at ganoon din para sa X stabilizer generator, mga error, at mga pagwawasto.
examples.suggested: kaya ganap silang hindi apektado/walang malay sa pareho, at ganoon din para sa X stabilizer generator, mga error, at mga pagwawasto.
examples.why: 'bigo' means 'failed', which reverses the intended sense: the generators are insensitive/unaffected ('oblivious'), not failing. A learner could conclude the stabilizers malfunction on Z errors, contradicting the point that detection/correction proceed independently.

examples.source: each parity check string halves the size of the code space / each stabilizer generator cuts the dimension of the code space in half.
examples.translation: ang bawat parity check string ay naghahatid ng kalahati ng laki ng code space / ang bawat stabilizer generator ay naghahatid ng kalahati ng dimensyon ng code space.
examples.suggested: ... ay hinahati sa kalahati ang laki/dimensyon ng code space.
examples.why: 'naghahatid ng kalahati' ('delivers half') is a calque that softens/obscures the active 'halves / cuts in half' claim; the reduction action is not clearly stated, only the resulting fraction.

examples.source: collectively they span the entire code space / which demonstrates that they span the code space.
examples.translation: sama-sama nilang nispanspan ang buong code space / na nagpapakita na nispanspan nila ang code space.
examples.suggested: sama-sama nilang sinasaklaw (ni-span) ang buong code space.
examples.why: 'nispanspan' is a malformed coinage from English 'span'; meaning is recoverable but a native technical writer would use 'saklaw' or 'ni-span'.

editor_note: This reads as native, well-edited Ukrainian technical prose, not machine translation. Domain terminology is correct and internally consistent: "квантова перевага" (quantum advantage), "кубіт", "гейт", "схема" (circuit), "пом'якшення помилок" (error mitigation), "відмовостійкі квантові обчислення" (FTQC), and English-kept terms (Sampler, Estimator, QPU, HPC, qLDPC) are all handled idiomatically; "залізо" is used consistently for "hardware" (an informal-but-standard Ukrainian tech register that fits the course). The register question is moot since both source and translation are entirely first-person plural ("ми") with no second-person address, so there are no Ви/ти slips. Meaning is faithful paragraph by paragraph through the final 40% — the two quantum-advantage criteria (i/ii), the QCSC vision, the Nighthawk/Loon/Starling/Blue Jay roadmap dates and qubit/gate counts, and the "marathon, not a sprint" summary all match. The only nuance worth noting is non-misleading: L23 renders "it will do so through the leveraging of quantum and HPC resources" as "для цього знадобиться спільне використання" (lit. "this will require"), a mild shift from descriptive to modal-necessity, but the intended meaning (advantage achieved via combined quantum+HPC resources) is fully preserved.

examples.source: quantum hardware ... hardware roadmap
examples.translation: квантового заліза ... Дорожня карта заліза
examples.why: "залізо" is informal-register slang for hardware; it is applied consistently to both quantum-hardware and the hardware roadmap, so it is a defensible, internally-consistent choice for this course's tone, not an inconsistency.

examples.source: it will do so through the leveraging of quantum and HPC resources together
examples.translation: для цього знадобиться спільне використання квантових та HPC-ресурсів
examples.why: English is descriptive ("will do so through"); Ukrainian uses "знадобиться" (will be required), a slight modal shift. Meaning (advantage achieved via combined resources) is preserved, so this does not mislead the learner.
examples.suggested: і що відбуватиметься це через спільне використання квантових та HPC-ресурсів

editor_note: This is faithful, natural Ukrainian technical prose that teaches as clearly as the English. The load-bearing quantities and directions of the superdense-coding protocol are all preserved: "передачу одного кубіта за допомогою двох класичних бітів" vs "передачу двох класичних бітів за допомогою одного кубіта," the e-bit cost, and the conditional gate steps (Якщо d=1 → вентиль Z, Якщо c=1 → вентиль X, with the "if 0, not" caveats intact). Bob's controlled-NOT control/target assignment and which measurement yields c vs d are correct, and the double negative around Holevo's theorem ("неможливо передати більше одного біту") and "це не означає, що ... не є цікавим, адже воно безумовно цікаве" are rendered without inversion. Domain terminology is conventional and consistent throughout: вентиль (gate), кубіт, заплутаність, стан Белла, вентиль Адамара, керований NOT, вимірювання в стандартному базисі. The text is third-person expository (Alice/Bob), so the informal-ти convention does not arise and there is no register risk. The only blemish is the slightly awkward instrumental-case construction "ділять одним e-бітом" (line 13) where accusative "ділять один e-біт" would read more naturally — a stylistic nit that does not mislead, hence PASS.

examples.source: we have a sender (Alice) and a receiver (Bob) that share one e-bit of entanglement
examples.translation: є відправник (Аліса) і отримувач (Боб), які ділять одним e-бітом заплутаності
examples.suggested: ...які ділять один e-біт заплутаності
examples.why: The verb 'ділити' here takes the instrumental 'одним e-бітом' where a native technical writer would use the accusative 'один e-біт'. Purely a grammatical-style nit; meaning (they share one e-bit) is unambiguous and not misleading.

editor_note: This is faithful, well-edited Ukrainian technical prose that teaches as clearly as the English. Core domain terminology is correct and used consistently throughout: вентиль (gate), схема (circuit), кубіт (qubit), обчислювальна вартість (computational cost), масштабування (scaling), цілочисельна факторизація, НСД (GCD), напівсуматор/повний суматор (half/full adder), нотація «O великого» (Big-O). The informal "ти" register holds consistently (розглянь, зауваж, уяви). I checked every meaning-bearing claim in the dense final 40% — the exponential-time-hypothesis negation ("жодна NP-повна задача не може мати алгоритм із субекспоненціальною вартістю"), the sub-exponential reservation for every ε>0, the trial-division worst-case O(2^{n/2}), the universality "places no requirements on cost" caveat, Grover's quadratic advantage for unstructured search, and the polynomial-vs-super-polynomial nuance — and all quantities, conditions, and caveats are preserved with no drift, inversion, softening, or hallucination. Two isolated stiff spots exist (the double-negative "не неспроможна факторизувати" on line 167, and "схеми зі цих вентилів" on line 125) but neither mislead, so they do not affect the verdict on this meaning-focused run.
